questions.question: How do social scientists define poverty beyond income and consumption?
questions.correct_answer: Through indicators like illiteracy, lack of healthcare, lack of job opportunities, and lack of safe drinking water/sanitation[cite: 1163, 1165].
questions.explanation: Modern analysis uses the Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) which includes health, education, and standard of living indicators[cite: 1166, 1167, 1168].
questions.source_section: Poverty as seen by social scientists

questions.question: What is 'Social Exclusion' in the context of poverty?
questions.correct_answer: A process where individuals or groups are excluded from facilities, benefits, and opportunities that others enjoy[cite: 1173].
questions.explanation: It involves the poor having to live only in poor surroundings with other poor people, excluded from social equality[cite: 1171].

questions.question: How is the 'Poverty Line' estimated in India?
questions.correct_answer: Based on a minimum level of food requirement, clothing, footwear, fuel, light, educational, and medical requirements[cite: 1194].
questions.explanation: The food requirement is based on desired calorie intake: 2400 calories per person/day in rural areas and 2100 in urban areas[cite: 1196, 1199].

questions.question: Which social groups are most vulnerable to poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Among economic groups, rural agricultural labour households and urban casual labour households are the most vulnerable[cite: 1310].

questions.question: What are the two typical cases/dimensions of poverty described in the text?
questions.correct_answer: Urban poverty (exemplified by Ram Saran) and Rural poverty (exemplified by Lakha Singh)[cite: 1107, 1133].
questions.explanation: Urban poverty often involves daily-wage labour in cities, while rural poverty involves landless labourers doing odd jobs for big farmers[cite: 1108, 1134, 1135].

questions.question: What is 'Vulnerability' to poverty?
questions.correct_answer: The greater probability of certain communities or individuals becoming or remaining poor in the future[cite: 1177].
questions.explanation: It is determined by options for alternative living and the ability to handle risks like natural disasters or job loss[cite: 1178, 1181].

questions.question: What were the historical causes of poverty in India?
questions.correct_answer: Low economic development under British colonial administration and the ruin of traditional handicrafts[cite: 1466, 1467].
questions.explanation: This was followed by high population growth and limited impact of the Green Revolution to only certain parts of India[cite: 1469, 1471].

questions.question: What are the two pillars of the current anti-poverty strategy?
questions.correct_answer: Promotion of economic growth and targeted anti-poverty programmes[cite: 1496].
questions.explanation: Economic growth provides resources for human development, while targeted schemes address specific poverty issues[cite: 1503, 1504, 1509].

questions.question: State the main objective of MGNREGA, 2005.
questions.correct_answer: To provide 100 days of wage employment to every household to ensure livelihood security in rural areas[cite: 1511].
questions.explanation: One-third of the proposed jobs are reserved for women[cite: 1513].

questions.question: What does the term 'Human Poverty' advocate?
questions.correct_answer: Broadening the concept of poverty beyond just food to include education, shelter, healthcare, and freedom from discrimination[cite: 1544, 1545, 1546].
questions.explanation: Income-based definitions capture only a limited part of what poverty truly means to people[cite: 1542].

notes.title: Calorie Requirements
notes.content: Rural areas require higher calories (2400) than urban areas (2100) due to more physical work[cite: 1199, 1200].

notes.title: Inter-State Disparities
notes.content: Poverty success rates vary; states like Punjab and Haryana reduced poverty through agricultural growth, Kerala through human resource development, and West Bengal through land reforms[cite: 1300, 1302, 1305].

notes.title: Global Poverty Standard
notes.content: The World Bank uses a uniform standard of $2.15 (PPP) per person per day to compare poverty across countries[cite: 1324].

facts.fact: The ratio of multidimensional poverty in India fell from 25% in 2015-16 to 15% in 2019-21[cite: 1103].
13.5 crore people escaped multidimensional poverty between 2015 and 2021[cite: 1218].
Bihar remains the poorest state with a Head Count Ratio (HCR) of 34%, while Kerala has the lowest at 1%[cite: 1255, 1294].
The United Nations' Sustainable Development Goal 1 aims to end poverty in all its forms everywhere by 2030[cite: 1421, 1428].
